Pertaining to a type of access to data in a database that is shared over a network. When you open a database in exclusive mode, you prevent others from opening the database.
A graphic or diagram that displays data or the relationships between sets of data in pictorial rather than numeric form.

The creation of an image containing geometric models, using color and shading to give the image a realistic look. Usually part of a geometric modeling package such as a CAD program, rendering uses mathematics to describe the location of a light source in relation to the object and to calculate the way in which the light would create highlights, shading, and variations in color. The degree of realism can range from opaque, shaded polygons to images approximating photographs in their complexity.
A pictorial graphic such as a digital photograph or illustration.
A folder that stores the user's graphics, e.g. digital pictures. Many graphic design and photo-editing programs use this location by default to open or save graphic files.
A picture-based sign-in method that authenticates a user by checking gestures made on a picture of the user's own choosing.

To duplicate information and reproduce it in another part of a document, in a different file or memory location, or in a different medium. A copy operation can affect data ranging from a single character to large segments of text, a graphics image, or from one to many data files.
A real-time communication session between two or more users. A session can involve IM, video, or audio.
A collection of communications (e-mail, IM, text messaging, voice mail, and so on) between two or more participants.
A secondary window that allows users to perform a command, asks users a question, or provides users with information or progress feedback.
A calendar item that includes reserved resources and/or invited attendees.
An email message that a user sends to others to invite them to a meeting.
The ‐ character, used to break a word between syllables at the end of a line or to separate the parts of a compound word.
An identifier used by app developers and advertising networks to provide more relevant advertising to users by understanding which apps they use and how they use them.
To remove a user's changes from the Microsoft Office Document Cache without deleting the file on the server or removing the latest version downloaded from the server from the Office Document Cache.
To scan a database, a list of files, or the Internet, either for a particular item or for anything that seems to be of interest.
Possessing improved, advanced, or more sophisticated features.
A DVD from which the video section can be played on the DVD set top box. When inserted in the PC, the user can access additional features such as games, web links etc.
A moveable object on the Start screen that opens apps or other customized content, like pinned websites.
A computer that provides shared resources, such as files or printers, to network users.
The percentage of a resource's capacity that is designated for a specific assignment.
A folder on another computer that has been made available for other people to use on the network.
The protocol or protocols operating at a particular level within a protocol suite, such as IP within the TCP/IP suite.
A named storage area on a computer containing files and other folders. Folders are used to organize information electronically, the same way actual folders in a filing cabinet do.

An operating system feature that allows executable routines (generally serving a specific function or set of functions) to be stored separately as files with .dll extensions. These routines are loaded only when needed by the program that calls them.
The replay of a digital media file.

Data that binds a sender's identity to the information being sent. A digital signature may be bundled with any message, file, or other digitally encoded information, or transmitted separately. Digital signatures are used in public key environments and provide authentication and integrity services.
A World Wide Web Consortium specification that describes the structure of dynamic HTML and XML documents in a way that allows them to be manipulated through a Web browser.
To change a system or a data file to make it more current.
To change an existing row of data in the data store or to transmit pending changes to the data store.
A computer monitor that supplements the main monitor.
An object (such as a customer, item, or employee) that shares a set of defined attributes or characteristics, and that is used to manage rules for data.
Data that is stored outside of the application that is currently in use.

The standard used to measure screen and printer resolution, expressed as the number of dots that a device can display or print per linear inch. The greater the number of dots per inch, the better the resolution.
Video images and sound stored in a digital format.

A form of storage characterized by high capacity (roughly 650 MB) and the use of laser optics rather than magnetic means for reading data. Optical disk storage commonly used for audio, video, and computer data. DVDs range in capacity from 4.7 GB to 17 GB, and can store significantly more than a CD-ROM.

The infrastructure that manages low-level graphics-related tasks, such as output adapter and swap chain configuration, and provides a common framework for managing virtualized graphics components and resources.
The expected total cost of a task or project, based on performance as of the status date. EAC is calculated as follows: EAC = ACWP + (BAC-BCWP)/CPI.
The process of maintaining connectivity outside of one's usual service or coverage area.
To transfer a copy of a file from a remote device to a requesting device by means of a modem or network.
An orientation of printed paper where the printed side is against a surface. For example, paper is face down when it is placed in a copier with the printed side against the copying surface.
A list that can be opened to reveal all choices for a given field.
An arrow associated with a drop-down combo or list box or some toolbar buttons, indicating a list the user can view by clicking the arrow.
A menu that drops from the menu bar when requested and remains open without further action until the user closes it or chooses a menu item.
A shaded outline that appears behind an object or text and creates the illusion of depth.
A form contained within another form or a report.
A complete Web site stored in a named subdirectory of the top-level Web site.
A title animation in Windows Movie Maker.

An antimalware driver that loads early in the boot process, to evaluate the safety of the other drivers being loaded. Based on a comparison against historical data for those drivers, the antimalware driver returns information to the kernel, which makes initialization decisions for each driver.
To activate or turn on.
A button on the address bar that enables the user to navigate to the specified location (such as a folder path or web address).
A keyboard key that sends the escape (ESC) character to the computer. Often this key moves the user back one level in the menu structure or exits the program.
In information processing, an operation performed by the computer at hand rather than by a remote computer.
In general, close at hand or restricted to a particular area.
A category of sensor that tracks environmental changes such as temperature, atmospheric pressure, humidity, wind speed and wind direction.
A network of computers, printers, and other devices located within a relatively limited area (for example, a building). A LAN enables any connected device to interact with any other on the network.
A networking standard that uses cables to provide network access. Ethernet is the most widely installed technology for connecting computers.
The timeframe a user specifies to suppress notifications from Windows.

A member of the TCP/IP suite of protocols, used to copy files between two computers on the Internet. Both computers must support their respective FTP roles: one must be an FTP client and the other an FTP server.
An orientation where the long dimension of a rectangular area (for example, screen or paper) is vertical.
The consistent vertical positioning of text, graphics, and other objects.
A UI element that users can click to display information about a selected object.
Attribute or characteristic of an object that is used to define its state, appearance, or value.
A grouping of properties presented as a tabbed page of a property sheet.
The activities and actions performed by customer service representatives to assist customers.
To fix an item, such as a tile, library, movie, game, or app, in a given area of the UI, so it is always accessible in that area (e.g., to make a library always visible in the navigation pane).
An Address Book that contains entries for every group, user, and contact within an organization's implementation of Exchange Server.
A path through which information passes between two computers or devices. It can refer to the physical medium (such as wires) or to a set of properties that distinguishes one channel from another.
A view of a document as it will appear when you print it.
One or more ranges of cells that you designate to print when you don't want to print the entire worksheet. If a worksheet includes a print area, only the print area is printed.
A value or condition that is not consistent with the true, specified, or expected value or condition.
Any fixed or removable objects that store computer data. Examples include hard disks, floppy disks, tapes, and compact discs.
A file that contains audio, video, or script data.
A server that hosts and delivers media across a network.
To quickly slide a finger for a short distance.
A gesture represented by a quick slide of a finger over a short distance.
A gesture represented by a quick slide of a finger over a short distance.
To trim the vertical or horizontal edges of an object. Pictures are often cropped to focus attention on a particular area.
A portion of a software window bounded by and separated from other portions by vertical or horizontal bars and having a separate function.
An application that contains a linked or embedded OLE object from another application.
A menu that is related to the active window or item, accessible by right-clicking the mouse.

The horizontal bar at the top of a window that displays the name of the window. Title bars can contain different buttons, such the Minimize, Maximize, and Close buttons, so that you can control how you want to view the window.
Of, pertaining to, or characterized by capital letters.
One or more identifying lines printed at the top of a page. A header may contain a page number, a date, the author's name, and the document title, the name of chapter.
To move the pointer over a UI element for a specified duration with the intent of interacting with that element.
One character (or more) that is slightly higher than other text on a line, such as a footnote reference mark.
A feature that a developer provides in an app for consumers to buy from within the app through an in-app purchase transaction.
A transaction performed by a consumer from within the context of an app to buy additional features for that app.
Audio, video, images, text, or any other information that is contained in a digital media file or stream.

An option on the Views menu in Windows Explorer that shows the most appropriate details for the file type in a flexible layout rather than only showing the details associated with the column headers in the view.
A group of reusable settings that describes the shared attributes and behaviors of a list item, a document, or a folder.
A physical item for sale or purchase or available for license.
A sequence of 25 letters and numbers. To activate some software, you must enter a product key during the activation process.
A limitation or a restriction.
A software setting that disables display rotation.
The button or slider on tablets and convertible tablets that rotates the display.

The special identification code printed as a set of vertical bars of differing widths on books, grocery products, and other merchandise. Used for rapid, error-free input in such facilities as libraries, hospitals, and grocery stores, bar codes represent binary information that can be read by an optical scanner. The coding can include numbers, letters, or a combination of the two; some codes include built-in error checking and can be read in either direction.
To move an item on the screen (1) by touching the item on a screen and then sliding the finger across the screen or (2) by pressing and holding down the left mouse button while moving the mouse.
A change made to a file or a document.
To make a change to an existing file or document.
To change between states or modes.
A keyboard key that, when pressed (and held), changes the actions of ordinary input.
Any device that changes electrical signals or computer data from one form to another. For example, an analog-to-digital converter translates analog signals to digital signals.
A UI command used to switch between accounts on a device.
A typographical symbol, such as a filled or empty circle, diamond, box, or asterisk, used to set off a small block of text or each item in a list.
A list in which each item or block of text is preceded by a bullet.
Hardware that transfers audio and video from an external source, such as a VCR or camcorder, to a computer.
Ready-made art, often appearing as a bitmap or a combination of drawn shapes.
A type of expansion board on PC-compatible computers that allows the playback and recording of sound, such as from a WAV or MIDI file or a music CD-ROM. Most PCs sold at retail include a sound card.
Pertaining to the chrome-free UI presentation mode that fills all or a portion of the display.
To end a user session for an Internet account. For example, you sign out of a Microsoft account, an Internet service provider account, or Skype.
To close a program or application.
A small overlap that extends the shape of the upper of two differently colored, abutting objects. A spread extends beyond the area that it knocks out.
An orientation where the long dimension of a rectangular area (for example, screen or paper) is horizontal.
To provide or give.
To move a document or other data in a window in order to view a particular portion of the document. Scrolling may be controlled by the mouse, arrow keys, or other keys on the keyboard.
To displace the left or right edge of a text item, such as a block or a line, relative to the margin or to another text item.
To move a task to a lower outline level [to the right] in the Task Name field. When you indent a task, it becomes a subtask of the nearest preceding task at a higher outline level.
Displacement of the left or right edge of a block of text in relation to the margin or to other blocks of text.
Text or symbols indicating that a computer program is waiting for input from the user.
To package your publication files by using the Pack and Go Wizard.

A group of files that modify Windows features. Package types include service packs, security updates, language packs, and hotfixes. Examples of packages include LanguagePack, Windows Foundation, and FeaturePack.

The work area on a computer screen that simulates the top of an actual desk. The desktop contains the Recycle Bin and other icons (shortcuts to programs, files, folders, and various types of documents such as letters, reports, or pictures) that you can arrange on the electronic desktop just as you would arrange real objects on top of a desk.
An app that runs on the desktop of Windows.
An app that runs on the desktop of Windows.
In a graphical user interface such as Windows, a pattern or picture in the screen background that can be chosen by the user.
An application that is meant to run on a desktop computer.
An independent section on a webpage. Each frame is actually a separate webpage and can have its own scrollbars.
A space, shown onscreen as a box, that contains a particular element of your publication.
One of many sequential images that make up video or animation.
The process of adding software to a computer system.
A disk, file, document, or other collection of information from which data is taken or moved.
A classification for grouping content by user-defined criteria such as page contents, file types, or a similar distinction.
A keyword or description used to identify groups of related items.
In communications, the portion of a Data Terminal Equipment (DTE) device that sends data.
A representation of facts, concepts or instructions in a formalized manner, suitable for communication, interpretation or processing.
A graphic element on a drawing that is associated with data.
Data from a table, form, query, view, or stored procedure, displayed in a row-and-column format.
A window that displays data from a table, form, query, view, or stored procedure in a row-and-column format.

A security feature that monitors programs on a computer to determine if they use system memory safely. To do this, DEP software works alone or with compatible microprocessors to mark some memory locations as non-executable." If a program tries to run code that is malicious or is not from a protected location
A property of a field that defines the kinds of data the field can store.
A field that contains the result of a query to an external data source.
In the Charts And Graphs template, a control handle on the Line Graph shape that you can drag to change the values in the graph.

A power-saving state that allows the computer to quickly resume full-power operation (typically within several seconds) when you want to start working again. All open documents and programs are saved to memory before the computer enters the low-power state.
For a computer system, to be in an inactive state, but not shut off. When the computer is switched back on, the desktop is restored exactly as it was.
A method of starting the computer that speeds boot time by ending the user session and hibernating the computer, rather than shutting down all running services.
To advance audio or video playback at a speed that is faster than normal.
To adjust horizontal spacing so that text is aligned evenly along both the left and right margins. Justifying text creates a smooth edge on both sides.
The consistent positioning of text, graphics, and other objects.
The uppermost directory on a computer, partition or volume.
The category of the top apps or games that are free at any point in time.
The category of the top apps or games that are not free at any point in time.
A marker that can be applied to content or items (like photos or text) to identify certain types of information. This allows the user to find, view and sort tagged items with ease.
A clickable UI element designed to look like a tab on a file folder. Tabs are usually arranged horizontally and allow users to switch between related pages or webpages.

A user interface element similar to a button that provides access to key Windows features, such as Start, search, sharing, devices, and settings. Charms differ from buttons in that they have extended functionality, such as flyout menus that provide access to individual or grouped commands.
A standard control that resembles a notebook and lets the user navigate different sections of information without leaving the current element on the screen.
A function of some Web browsers that allows users to surf and view multiple pages by loading Web sites into sections (or tabs) of one page, rather than multiple pages.
To take action on an email message by either replying to the sender, to the sender and all recipients, or by forwarding the email to someone else.
An artificial language that specifies instructions to be executed on a computer. The term covers a wide spectrum, from binary-coded machine language to high-level languages.
To carry on a real-time conversation with other users by computer.
A collection of binaries that can be installed on top of the core product and enables users to select a preferred language so that the user interface and Help files appear in that preferred language.
Add-in software that provides partially localized user interfaces for particular computer programs in languages not supported by localized versions of those programs.
An external file included with an e-mail message.

A data type that will provide support for storing binary files within an Access database. More similar to the SharePoint concept of attachments than the current OLE object fields in Access. The data will be stored as a raw blob within the database.
Engaging in fraud or deceptive.
A unit of computer memory equal to 1,024 megabytes (1,024 1,048,576, [230] bytes).
A single position or item in a dimension. Dimension members can be user-defined or predefined and can have properties associated with them.
A person who is part of a group.

A distribution mechanism for frequently-updated content that is aggregated by an RSS reader and delivered or published in a standard XML format. The content is often summarized, with links to a more complete version.
An application that comes with a product key and DVD that allows users them to purchase Windows and activate their current copy of Windows from the client.

A pointing device used mainly but not exclusively for computer games. A joystick has a base, on which control buttons can be mounted, and a vertical stem, which the user can move in any direction to control the movement of an object on the screen.

A series of items arranged vertically within some type of framework--for example, a continuous series of cells running from top to bottom in a spreadsheet, a set of lines of specified width on a printed page, a vertical line of pixels on a video screen, or a set of values aligned vertically in a table or matrix.
The area in each row of a database table that stores the data value for some attribute of the object modeled by the table.
A unit of computer memory equal to 1,024 megabytes (1,024 1,048,576, [230] bytes).
Having a frequency in the electromagnetic spectrum in the range just below that of red light.
Light that is beyond red in the color spectrum. While the light is not visible to the human eye, infrared transmitters and receivers can send and receive infrared signals.
A method of protecting the rights of an originator of a creative work, such as a text, a piece of music, a painting, or a computer program, through law.
Personal Area Networks (PAN) that communicate directly between one or more Bluetooth-enabled computers or devices.
The process of turning data into a stream of bits. Encoding is part of the serialization process that converts data into a stream of ones and zeros.
Confidence in the reliability of an entity or service and the information that it provides.
The process in which a developer tests an app to make sure it meets app requirements.
To ensure that all data sources that are used by a KPI or scorecard are available.
To verify, test or control by comparison or examination.
A table, chart, graphic, equation, or other form of information.
A feature of Windows that checks for problems associated with network connections.
A distinctive symbol that identifies you, your business, or your organization. It can combine a name, motto, and graphics.
The digital persona" by which other users recognize and interact with you."

The process of viewing and updating the actual progress of tasks so that you can see progress across time, evaluate slippage of tasks, compare scheduled or baseline data to actual data, and check the completion percentage of tasks and your project.
The feature that blocks third-party web content that could potentially track someone's web activity.
To follow the flow of information; to monitor progress.
An exponentiation operator used in some programming languages. For example, the expression 3^2 represents the number 3 raised to the second power.
The small, upward-pointing symbol (^) typically found over the 6 key on the top row of a microcomputer keyboard.
A symbol used to represent the Control key on the keyboard. For example, ^Z means hold the Control key down and press the Z key.""
A unique identifier of an object or person.
The process of rewriting parts of a file to contiguous sectors on a hard disk to increase the speed of access and retrieval.
To copy files from a backup storage device to their normal location, especially if the files are being copied to replace files that were accidentally lost or deleted.
A visual design surface.
The portion of InfoPath used to design forms.
To translate all the source code of a program from a high-level language into object code prior to execution of the program.
The location where text or graphics will be inserted. Also used for text box controls to indicate input focus.
A list of options from which a user can choose. The options can be represented as text or graphics. A menu can contain other menus (submenus).

A rectangular bar displayed in an application program's on-screen window, often at the top, from which menus can be selected by the user. Names of available menus are displayed in the menu bar; choosing one with the keyboard or with a mouse causes the list of options in that menu to be displayed.
A group of OneNote sections.
Any self-contained piece of work created with an application program and, if saved on disk, given a unique filename by which it can be retrieved.
Properties, such as title, subject, and author, that are stored with each data access page.
In windowing environments, such as the Apple Macintosh and Microsoft Windows, an on-screen window (enclosed work area) in which the user can create, view, or work on a document.
A location on a SharePoint site where a collection of files and their associated metadata are stored.
A page where there is no information displayed
The exchange of text messages and computer files over a communications network, such as a local area network or the Internet.

A type of object within a process that runs program instructions. Using multiple threads allows concurrent operations within a process and enables one process to run different parts of its program on different processors simultaneously. A thread has its own set of registers, its own kernel stack, a thread environment block, and a user stack in the address space of its process.
A collection of contact information for particular individuals, groups or organizations.
A condition in which paper is blocked in the printing path of a printer, scanner, photocopier or fax machine.
Any self-contained piece of work created with an application program and, if saved on disk, given a unique filename by which it can be retrieved.
The size of the sheet of paper on which you print the publication, before trimming.
The location to which products that have been ordered should be sent.
A number specifying a location in memory where data is stored.

The path to an object, document, file, page, or other destination. An address can be a URL (Web address) or a UNC path (network address), and can include a specific location within a file, such as a bookmark within a document or a cell range within a spreadsheet.

A string that identifies a user so that the user can receive Internet email. An email address on the Internet typically consists of an account name, followed by the @ (at) symbol, a host name, and a domain name.
A unique identifier that identifies a network node to other nodes on the network.
The feature that allows users to navigate directly to the webpage of the top-ranked search result when a search is performed in the address bar.
Header and footer information, or header information only, which encapsulates or precedes document data for transport.
The paper that a publication is printed on.

To transfer information either to a storage device, such as a disk, or to an output device, such as the monitor or a printer. Writing is the means by which a computer provides the results of processing. A computer can also be said to write to the screen when it displays information on the monitor.
A control that indicates whether or not an option is selected. A check mark or x" appears in the box when the option is selected."
To prevent the writing (recording) of information, usually on a disk.
Pertaining to a disk to which content cannot be recorded.
To create an object, such as an email message.
A letter, number, punctuation mark, or other symbol.
To move around in a document.
A continuous sequence of characters that share a common format.
A recognition feature that helps increase input speed and reduce effort by providing users with word completion and next word prediction alternates as the user is writing/typing.
The distance between characters in a line of text. Tracking, kerning, and scaling can be used to adjust the space between characters.

A grouping of alphabetic, numeric, and other characters that have some relationship in common. For example, the standard ASCII character set includes letters, numbers, symbols, and control codes that make up the ASCII coding scheme.
A set of characters added to the end of a file name that identifies the file type or format.

A device that includes an antenna, a radio receiver, and a processor for use with the worldwide GPS (Global Positioning System). A GPS receiver uses position and time information from four GPS satellites to calculate precise information about its current location, its speed of travel, and the current time.
The ` character.
The ` character.
To join or link.
To assign a drive letter, port, or computer name to a shared resource so that you can use it.
An actor's interface component through which actors communicate with other actors.
To change a program in order to be able to run it on a different computer.

A hardware- or software-based interface used to transfer information between a computer and other devices. Hardware ports are physical connections that are visible on the outside of the computer. Software ports are the numbered gateways in programs that software programs use to exchange information.
Any one-dimensional (1-D) shape that can be glued between two shapes in a drawing to connect the shapes.
A link via wire, radio, fiber-optic cable, or other medium between two or more communications devices.
A connection between an OLE object and its OLE server, or between a dynamic data exchange (DDE) source document and a destination document.
An area that shows available network connections and helps users quickly connect to the most appropriate one.
The state that enables a PC to turn on instantly and have tiles and other services update even when the screen is off.
Any token that a program can use to identify and access an object such as a device, a file, a window, or a dialog box.
A user interface control that facilitates modification of an object's appearance, position, or behavior.

A display format, like that of Windows, that represents a program's functions with graphic images such as buttons and icons. GUIs enable a user to perform operations and make choices by pointing and clicking with a mouse.
A 16-byte value generated from the unique identifier on a device, the current date and time, and a sequence number. It is used to identify a particular device, component, user, or session.
A person who uses a computer.

A security feature that helps prevent unauthorized changes to a computer, by asking user for permission or administrator credentials before performing actions that could potentially affect the computer's operation or that change settings that affect multiple users.
A file that contains configuration information for a specific user, such as desktop settings, persistent network connections, and application settings.
A unique name that identifies a user account to Windows. An account's user name must be unique among the other group names and user names within its own domain or workgroup.
A set of instructions that a computer uses to perform a specific task, such as word processing, accounting, or data management.
The operational state of a computer or a program. For example, edit mode is the state in which a program accepts changes to a file.
A placeholder on the Start screen that represents an app not yet installed on the PC.
A runtime environment for Windows Store apps that's designed to minimize app impact on the computer by placing runtime restrictions on what apps can do.
A UI element that appears along the top or bottom edge of the screen and may include app commands or navigation.
A feature of Internet Explorer 10 that allows users to easily switch between websites and their corresponding apps.
A Windows Store service that enables end users to update apps one at a time or all at the same time for all apps purchased through the Windows Store.

Collected data about an app, such as analytics of download, usage, in-application transaction, customer ratings provided by the Windows Store developer portal. This type of information helps developers clearly understand how their applications are doing in the Windows market.
A single-threaded apartment the thread of which is the core UI thread for an application window.
The name of a product in the store that appears to customers. To the end user, this is the name of the product.
A function relevant to an app in use that is displayed on the app bar.
The functionality that allows users to switch back to recently used apps.
To execute a group of statements repeatedly.
A series of instructions that is repeated a fixed number of times or until a terminating condition is reached.

A property of a replica that indicates which members of the replica set it can synchronize with and which conflict resolution rules apply. Replicas fall into three visibility types: global, local, and anonymous.
The ability of one element to see or refer to another. For one element to send a message to another element, the latter must be visible to the former.

A set of conditions and associated actions that may be applied to certain tasks. For example, an email rule might stipulate that all messages from a particular sender be placed into a specific folder.
A pattern or mask through which data is passed to separate specified items.
To display only the rows in a list that satisfy the conditions you specify. You use the AutoFilter command to display rows that match one or more specific values, calculated values, or conditions.
A device that shares its Internet connection with other Wi-Fi-enabled devices.
A sequence of shades ranging from black through white, used in computer graphics to add detail to images or to represent a color image on a monochrome output device.
A filter effect in Windows Movie Maker.
Text that does not fit within a text box. The text is hidden until it can be flowed into a new text box, or until the text box it overflows is resized to include it.
Pertaining to an entire document, file, or program rather than to a restricted segment of it.
Resolving resource conflicts or overallocations by delaying or splitting certain tasks. When Project levels a resource, its selected assignments are distributed and rescheduled.

The electronic components that generate the video signal sent through a cable to a video display. The video adapter is usually located on the computer's main system board or on an expansion board, but it is sometimes built into the terminal.
A visual display of data in a dashboard that can be coordinated with other report views by using filters.
To show something.
A descriptive or user-friendly label for a control or object, displayed on the screen. The display name may differ from the internal name.
A window that appears when an option is selected. Typically, the window remains visible until the mouse button is released.
To emphasize displayed characters, as by displaying them in reverse video (light on dark rather than dark on light, and vice versa) or against a colored background.
A visual output device aimed at displaying various type of information.
A name of a person as it is shown in a user interface.
A demonstration of a product or service that a customer interacts with to see how features work.
A database object that prints information formatted and organized according to your specifications.
The process of exporting a virtual machine and its snapshots while the virtual machine is running.
Not visible to the user.
Pertaining to an item that is only visible to the owner.
A utility or feature that aids in accomplishing a task or set of tasks.

A row, column, or block of buttons or icons, usually displayed across the top of the screen, that represent tasks or commands within the program. The toolbar buttons provide shortcuts to common tasks frequently accessed from the menus.
A command button used in a toolbar (or status bar).
A device, such as a disk drive, printer, modem, or joystick, that is connected to a computer and is controlled by the computer's microprocessor.
To make a transmission appear to come from a user other than the user who performed the action.
The writing or drawing strokes that you make with your stylus.
The process of using a tablet pen to create drawings or handwriting.
The likelihood of a computer system or device continuing to function over a given period of time and under specified conditions.
In respect to privacy, an individual's ability to view, modify, and contest the accuracy and completeness of PII collected about him or her. Access is an element of the Fair Information Practices.
To gain entry to memory in order to read or write data.

Authorization to perform operations associated with a specific shared resource, such as a file, directory, or printer. Permissions must be granted by the system administrator to individual user accounts or administrative groups.

The quality of a system incorporating hardware or software to engage a flexible, customizable user interface, alternative input and output methods, and greater exposure of screen elements to make the computer usable by people with cognitive, hearing, physical, or visual disabilities.
A device used to connect wireless computers and other wireless devices to a wired network.
Any combination of keys that must be pressed sequentially.

To copy driver binaries, install services, and add registry keys for one or more drivers from a driver package onto a system, without the context of a specific device. A system can be booted from reflected drivers, however full device installation may still be required in order to enable full driver functionality.
Text that alerts a user to a note or a warning.
To halt a process or action, typically without restoring the prior state.
In I/O operations, to prevent execution of a thread.
To write data (typically a file) to a storage medium.
To start or reset a computer by turning the power on, by pressing a reset button on the computer case, or by issuing a software command to restart.
The charm that takes users to the Start screen.
To activate an application program (especially on the Macintosh) from the operating system's user interface.
To press and release a mouse button once without moving the mouse. Clicking is usually performed to select or deselect an item or to activate a program or program feature.
To place a finger on the screen and hold it there for a part of a second or more.
A gesture represented by placing a finger on the screen and holding it there for a part of a second or more
To pick a command or an option from within a graphical user interface, as by clicking a button in a dialog box or pulling down a menu and then releasing the mouse button on one of its options.
To tap a device screen and hold one's finger or stylus in place.
A contiguous on-screen keyboard.
To wait for incoming traffic, calls or connection requests on a port.
A menu item with options that can be applied to a file before it is shared with others (e.g. 'Mark as Final' or 'Add a Digital Signature').
In the printing and display of characters on the screen, an imaginary horizontal line with which the base of each character, excluding descenders, is aligned.
A link that takes you to a precise location, such as a slide within a presentation, a section of a document, or a specific page within a site, instead of to the main page of the web site.
An app that is included in the product when a user first starts using it.
One character (or more) printed slightly below the baseline of surrounding text.
A notebook that is smaller in size and lighter in weight than a full-sized notebook, while retaining the same functionality.
To insert information created in one program, such as a chart, an equation, an image, a video or a font, into another program or Web page.
A type of drop-down menu that appears when a user points to a command on a higher-level menu.
To be unresponsive.
The test automation framework used to certify devices or systems for Windows.
A digital interface specification for delivering digital content and connecting consumer electronic devices.
To associate a flag with a message or other item.
A graphic that indicates follow-up action is required, or otherwise identifies an important item.
To place at least two fingers on an object and rotate your fingers in a clockwise or counterclockwise direction.
A gesture represented by placing at least two fingers on an object and rotating your fingers in a clockwise or counterclockwise direction.
A filter effect in Windows Movie Maker.
A gesture represented by placing at least two fingers on an object and rotating your fingers in a clockwise or counterclockwise direction.
To remove part of a document, usually placing it temporarily in memory so that the cut portion can be inserted (pasted) elsewhere.
To cause an application to display information on a computer screen.
To acknowledge an action or the value of some data (e.g. password) by definite assurance.
The UI label for the button on the app bar that affixes a tile to the Start screen.
To transfer a file from one location to another. After the file is transferred, there is only one copy of the file, in the new location.
To take out objects from a list, database, or other item, or to uninstall a program.
An option that isolates the foreground portion of an image and make the background transparent.
To make a single file out of a set of files using a program such as compress, gzip, or PKZIP.
To copy files to a recordable CD.
To turn a setting off or to remove a value.
To move an item to a higher level within a tree structure.
To reverse playback of a digital media file at a speed that is faster than normal.
To continue to press a keyboard key or mouse button.

A feature that enables records managers to create and manage entries to a “Holds list” such that any document, image, or webpage on that list cannot be deleted, modified, or allowed to expire until the item is removed from the list. The feature is typically used for items that are subject to events such as litigation, audits, or investigations.
To modify the value of a property or attribute.
To put new data in the place of other data, usually after conducting a search for the data to be replaced. Text-based applications such as word processors typically include Find and Replace commands.
To terminate a session with a computer accessed through a communications line usually a computer that is both distant and open to many users.
An action that removes redundant messages from an e-mail conversation to reduce information overload and improve readability.
To remove unused space from data tables.
To cause text or an image to visually fade.
To reverse something or change it to its opposite.
A navigation button used to complete and close a wizard.
An item on the File menu that writes data (typically a file) to a storage medium.
To transfer into a store or storage location.
To retain a physical representation of (data or instructions) that enables them to be subsequently retrieved.
To end an application's relationship with an open file so that the application will no longer be able to access the file without opening it again.
To make an object, such as a file, accessible.
A button that opens Sync Center.
To enlarge a selected portion of a graphical image or document to fill a window or the screen.
When referring to software, to load the program and installation files onto your computer so that the program runs correctly.
When referring to hardware, to physically connect the device to your computer, to load device drivers onto your computer, and to configure device properties and settings.
To place information from storage into memory for processing, if it is data, or for execution, if it is program code.
To update displayed information with current data.
To reset a system using a method that restores PC settings to defaults but retains a user's files and personalization touches.
To reset the Windows operating system using a method that restores PC settings to defaults but retains a user's files and personalization touches.
To locate text or a specific item on a Web page or in a document.

To detect, locate, and correct logical or syntactical errors in a program or malfunctions in hardware. In hardware contexts, the term troubleshoot is the term more often used, especially when the problem is a major one.
To find.
To include an external document, a file, or an executable program with an e-mail message.
To expose a virtual disk to the user, backed by a file.
To ask someone to engage in an instant conversation.
The set of features that let employees use personal devices to access corporate information and services and let IT admins manage such devices.
To stop the operation of a program or execution of a command temporarily.
To gain access to a specific computer, program, or network by identifying oneself with a username and a password.
To confirm either that a result is correct or that a procedure or sequence of operations has been performed.
To add new text, objects or cells without overwriting existing data.

An application of the Standard Generalized Markup Language that uses tags to mark elements, such as text and graphics, in a document to indicate how Web browsers should display these elements to the user and should respond to user actions.
The Internet protocol used to deliver information over the World Wide Web.
The Internet protocol used to deliver information over the World Wide Web.
To break a connection.
To put into operation or to use.
To reduce the amount of information displayed by hiding from view the subentries within a folder, section or conversation, or a portion of text or other content within a control.
The complementary tasks of gathering and distributing data. Input is data that is acquired from a device or entered by the user through a device. Output is data that is sent to a device.
To repost a tweet from another Twitter user, sharing the tweet with one's own followers.
A unique identifier of an object or person.

A SmartArt graphic layout used to compare or show the relationship between two ideas. Each of the first two lines of Level 1 text corresponds to text at the top of one side of the center point. Emphasizes Level 2 text, which is limited to four shapes on each side of the center point. The balance tips towards the side with the most shapes containing Level 2 text. Unused text does not appear, but remains available if you switch layouts.

In object-oriented programming, a language that lets a program or object written in one language communicate with another program written in an unknown language. An IDL is used to define interfaces between client and server programs. For example, an IDL can provide interfaces to remote CORBA objects.
A data type used to hold date and time information.
A small part, amount, or proportion of something.
A number that is not a whole number, such as ½ (vulgar fraction) or 0.5 (decimal fraction), formed by dividing one quantity into another.
The range and depth of a search on a portal site, desktop or other.
An arrangement of search results ordered by relevance using a special search algorithm.
An assessment of music or video content on a scale that corresponds to the user's opinion of the content.
A portion of a line, defined by its beginning and ending points.
To change the dimensions of a shape by dragging one of its handles after it has been selected with the pointer tool.
A part of the user interface dedicated to a particular purpose, such as instant message area.""
A type of chart that displays the trend of values over time or categories. By displaying the sum of the plotted values, an area chart also shows the relationship of parts to a whole.
A set of services used by Windows Phone, Windows, and other applications to access the user's current location.
A structural attribute of a cube that organizes data into levels. For example, a Geography dimension might include the members Country, Region, State or Province, and City.
In an Active Directory forest, a server that contains a writable copy of the Active Directory database, participates in Active Directory replication, and controls access to network resources.
A set of records defined by a table, a query, or an SQL expression.

A collection of computers in a networked environment that share a common database, directory database, or tree. A domain is administered as a unit with common rules and procedures, which can include security policies, and each domain has a unique name.
The set of possible values that you can specify for an independent variable in a function, or for a database attribute.

A value that developers specify in the app manifest for their Windows Store apps. Windows Store apps use capabilities to declare that the app requires programmatic access to protected user resources, such as the Pictures library, or connected devices, such as a webcam.

A handler that displays a lightweight, read-only preview of a file’s contents without launching the application associated with the file. For example, a preview handler displays a Shell item in the Windows Explorer preview pane.

An ad that is served to a user's screen either as a pushed (sent by e-mail) or pulled ad (requested by a user's browser). This includes measurement of responses from an ad delivery system to a user's browser.
A preliminary version.
The color that is currently selected for drawing or displaying text on the screen. In monochrome displays, the foreground color is the color of a bitmap or other graphic.

An antimalware driver that loads early in the boot process, to evaluate the safety of the other drivers being loaded. Based on a comparison against historical data for those drivers, the antimalware driver returns information to the kernel, which makes initialization decisions for each driver.

An antimalware driver that loads early in the boot process, to evaluate the safety of the other drivers being loaded. Based on a comparison against historical data for those drivers, the antimalware driver returns information to the kernel, which makes initialization decisions for each driver.
A protocol that establishes the security association and shared keys necessary for computers to communicate by using Internet Protocol security (IPsec).
The combination of hardware (touchscreen or touchpad) and software elements that provides a way to control programs by touching multiple points on the screen simultaneously.
A real-time, physical action applied to an object, in which multiple contact points are used simultaneously.
An extension of the conventional touch input feature to allow multiple fingers touching the computer screen at the same time.
Pertaining to the delivery of information that combines different content formats, such as video, audio, still images, graphics, animation, and text.
A type of flowchart that shows the relationship between a business process and the functional units (such as departments) responsible for that process.
A sequence of words or other text used to gain access to a network, program, or data. A passphrase is generally longer for added security.
An item on the Help menu that opens the About dialog box, which includes the copyright, licensing, and license terms for the product.
A tool that lets you enter complex characters and symbols, such as those used in East Asian written languages, using a standard keyboard.
The process of viewing and interacting with an existing Remote Desktop Protocol session initiated by another user.
A Windows feature that allows users to connect remotely to a computer and access programs, files, and network resources as though they were sitting at the computer.
The app that allows users to connect remotely to a computer and access programs, files, and network resources as though they were sitting at the computer.
The client software that enables users to connect to a remote computer that has the Remote Desktop feature enabled or to a remote desktop server.
A networking feature that lets one computer directly place information into the memory of another computer.
To adjust the perspective of an image or document in order to view a larger area on the screen at one time, as if viewed from farther away.

Part of the integrated Routing and Remote Access service that provides remote networking for telecommuters, mobile workers, and system administrators who monitor and manage servers at multiple branch offices. Users can use Network Connections to dial in to remotely access their networks for services such as file and printer sharing, electronic mail, scheduling, and SQL database access.
The collection of networking and system components that lets devices turn on instantly, keep apps and data up to date even when devices are sleeping, and use batteries more efficiently.
A natural number, zero, or the negative of a natural number.
The Microsoft web browser.
The touch-optimized, immersive web browser for Windows.
A nonfinal release of Internet Explorer 10.
The web browser component of Windows that provides a traditional and familiar browsing experience.
A routable protocol in the TCP/IP protocol suite that is responsible for IP addressing, routing, and the fragmentation and reassembly of IP packets.
A binary number that uniquely identifies a host (computer) connected to the Internet to other Internet hosts, for the purposes of communication through the transfer of packets.

A business that supplies Internet connectivity services to individuals, businesses, and other organizations. An ISP provides a telephone number, a user name, a password, and other connection information so that users can access the Internet through the ISP's computers.

An Intel microprocessor that uses explicitly parallel instruction set computing and 64-bit memory addressing. Itanium-based" refers to systems or platforms that are based on the Itanium processor. "Itanium2-based" refers to systems or platforms that are based on the Itanium2 processor. "Itanium architecture-based" refers to systems or platforms that are based on the Itanium and Itanium2 processors."
The category of apps and games that are suggested to users due to their recent popularity.
The category of apps and similar items that have recently become available.
Pertaining to a mixture of characters that are read from left to right and characters that are read from right to left.
Pertaining to a mixture of characters that are read from left to right and characters that are read from right to left.
A computer that has properties both of a tablet and a laptop.

To reconcile the differences between files, e-mail, appointments, and other items stored on one computer or device with versions of the same files on another computer. Once the differences are determined, both sets of items are updated.
An item on the Control Panel that provides user a comprehensive view over all roamed content, as well as ability to check on the synchronization progress of individual items.

To reconcile the differences between files, e-mail, appointments, and other items stored on one computer or device with versions of the same files on another computer. Once the differences are determined, both sets of items are updated.
The pairing of two or more actions that are performed together as a single action; the action succeeds or fails as a whole.
The process of reconciling the differences between data stored in one location and a copy of the same data stored in another location.
The process of reconciling the differences between data stored in one location and a copy of the same data stored in another location.
A program, routine, or process that performs a specific system function to support other programs.

A business that supplies Internet connectivity services to individuals, businesses, and other organizations. An ISP provides a telephone number, a user name, a password, and other connection information so that users can access the Internet through the ISP's computers.

A tested, cumulative set of all hotfixes, security updates, critical updates and updates, as well as additional fixes for problems found internally since the release of the product. Service packs may also contain a limited number of customer requested design changes or features, and contain all fixes since the previous service pack or RTM.
The width-to-height ratio of a video or still image, or of the screen on which an image is displayed.
Pertaining to tasks that need to be done.

The bar that contains the 'Start' button and appears by default at the bottom of the desktop. You can click the taskbar buttons to switch between programs. You can also hide the taskbar, move it to the sides or top of the desktop, and customize it in other ways.

A button that represents an item or program that is open and running on your computer. For example, if you open Microsoft Word, and then minimize it, it will be displayed as a taskbar button, which you can then click when you want to maximize it.

The software that controls the allocation and usage of hardware resources such as memory, central processing unit (CPU) time, disk space, and peripheral devices. The operating system is the foundation on which programs are built.
A holding place for a group of linked documents or Works tasks / templates related to a single user activity.
A SmartArt graphic layout type that includes layouts designed to show steps in a process or timeline.
The degree of excellence, or the desired standards, in a product, process, or project.
To set up hardware and software.
Any significant occurrence in the system or an application that requires a user to be notified or an entry to be added to a log.
An action or occurrence to which a program might respond. Examples include state changes, data transfers, key presses, and mouse movements.
A customer service issue or problem reported by a customer and the activities that customer service representatives use to resolve it.
Of, pertaining to, or characteristic of an action or operation that takes place based on whether or not a certain condition is true.
An object-oriented programming language, developed by Sun Microsystems, Inc.
To eliminate text, a file, or part of a document with the intention of removing the information permanently.

Capable of using or being displayed on the full area of a display screen. Applications running in windowing environments, although they might use the entire area of the screen, commonly allocate different areas to different windows, any of which can be enlarged to fill the entire screen.
A software package that includes all features and does not require a previous version of the relevant software to be installed on the user's computer.
The process in which Microsoft tests an app to make sure it meets app requirements.
A touchpad that provides a consistent, accurate, and responsive experience for users.
A process that verifies whether installed software is genuine.
The accuracy of data and its conformity to its expected value, especially after being transmitted or processed.
An outlined container that displays a markup element, such as a comment or revision, in the margin of a document.
A small pop-up window that informs users of a non-critical problem or special condition in a control.

A type of program consisting of a set of instructions to an application or tool program. A script usually expresses instructions by using the application`s or tool`s rules and syntax, combined with simple control structures such as loops and if/then expressions.
Any combination of operators, constants, literal values, functions, and names of fields (columns), controls, and properties that evaluates to a single value.

The ability of a word-processing program or a text-editing program to break lines of text automatically to stay within the page margins or window boundaries of a document without the user having to do so with carriage returns, as is typically necessary on a typewriter.
A device that is added to the base computer system to increase functionality, such as audio, networking, graphics, or SCSI controller.
To join or combine thereby increasing the quantity or size of something.
An auxiliary software component that extends or enhances the features of other software.
The smallest executable entity within a programming language.
A set of graphics, text, and colors for defining different levels of performance when comparing an actual value and a target value within a KPI.
A key that accompanies a graphic and explains how information is presented.
A feature that audibly reads the text on the screen aloud and describes some events to users.

The meaning of data as it is intended to be interpreted by people. Data consists of facts, which become information when they are seen in context and convey meaning to people. Computers process data without any understanding of what the data represents.
The information provided with a product by a manufacturer describing how to use a product.
A Microsoft Web site that provides customers with access to a database of articles written by Microsoft Support professionals.
A searchable collection of articles and facts about a particular subject area.
A data unit of 1024 bytes.

The core of layered architecture that manages the most basic operations of the operating system and the computer's processor. The kernel schedules different blocks of executing code, called threads, for the processor to keep it as busy as possible and coordinates multiple processors to optimize performance. The kernel also synchronizes activities among Executive-level subcomponents, such as I/O Manager and Process Manager, and handles hardware exceptions and other hardware-dependent functions. The kernel works closely with the hardware abstraction layer.
A data unit of 1024 bytes.
A network of computers, printers, and other devices located within a relatively limited area (for example, a building). A LAN enables any connected device to interact with any other on the network.

A method used with SCSI and IDE disk drives to translate the cylinder, head, and sector specifications of the drive into addresses that can be used by an enhanced BIOS. LBA is used with drives that are larger than 528MB.

A type of display that uses a liquid compound having a polar molecular structure, sandwiched between two transparent electrodes. When an electric field is applied, the molecules align with the field, forming a crystalline arrangement that polarizes the light passing through it. A polarized filter laminated over the electrodes blocks polarized light. In this way, a grid of electrodes can selectively turn on" a cell

The primary access protocol for Active Directory. LDAP is an industry-standard protocol, established by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), that allows users to query and update information in a directory service. Active Directory supports both LDAP version 2 and LDAP version 3.

A standard international numeric abbreviation for a country or geographical region. A language identifier is a 16-bit value that consists of a primary language identifier and a secondary language identifier.
Add-in software that provides partially localized user interfaces for particular computer programs in languages not supported by localized versions of those programs.
The actual software bits available to developers to enable them to use the Live Connect program.
Usually 1,048,576 bytes. It is sometimes interpreted as 1 million bytes.
Usually 1,048,576 bytes. It is sometimes interpreted as 1 million bytes.
Information about the properties or structure of data that is not part of the values the data contains.
A file that stores an image as graphical objects (lines, circles, polygons) rather than as pixels. Metafiles preserve an image more accurately than pixels when the image is resized.
In object-oriented programming, a named code block that performs a task when called.
A number used to indicate the cost of a route in the Internet Protocol (IP) routing table that enables the selection of the best route among possible multiple routes to the same destination.
The public multinational corporation that develops, manufactures, licenses, and supports a wide range of products and services predominantly related to computing.

column5: Microsoft ᎠᏓᎵᏍᎪᎸᏓᏁᎯ ᏕᎪᏪᎸᎢ & ᎢᏯᏛᏁᎵᏓᏍᏗ ᎾᎿ ᎾᎾᏛᏁᎲ
column10: Applications in Windows Store are distributed with user-based licensing model. Windows runtime and application model will only allow applications that are for user-based license as well as signed by Windows Store to be installed/run on Windows 8.

The website where Microsoft provides users with access to a variety of resources, information about software, services and other merchandise that may or may not be available for purchase, download areas, software and tools.
A physical store where Microsoft sells recommended software and devices.
A single set of sign-in credentials (email address and password) that provides user access to PCs running Windows and Live-connected services.
In Windows 8 all windows portal experiences support and persist Live ID logins streamlining the partner experience when working with multiple services and sites.
The website hosted by Microsoft where users can browse community-approved wiki articles, post questions and answers or start a discussion on technology-related topics.
The public multinational corporation that develops, manufactures, licenses, and supports a wide range of products and services predominantly related to computing.

A COM-based multimedia platform and infrastructure for digital media in Microsoft Windows. It focuses on content protection, audio and video quality, and interoperability for digital rights management (DRM).
The set of look-and-feel elements (type, color, icons, etc.) that create a sense of brand and a cohesive user experience with Microsoft products.
An online community that helps you choose how to respond to potential malware threats, and that helps to stop the spread of new malware infections.
An app that facilitates online file storage.
The year 2010 edition of the Microsoft messaging and collaboration client software for personal computers accessing Microsoft Exchange Server-based email and collaboration services.

A virtual community that provides people with opportunities to interact (via blogs, chats, newsgroups, webcasts, user groups, and forums) with Microsoft employees, experts, and peers in order to share knowledge and news about Microsoft technology.

A website from Microsoft which provides updates (patches and fixes) for multiple Microsoft products in one place, including Windows operating system software and Windows-based hardware, Microsoft Office system, Microsoft SQL Server, and Microsoft Exchange Server.
A Microsoft site that provides a comprehensive listing of updates that can be distributed over a corporate network.
A Web site from Microsoft which provides solutions for the top issues about Windows Update or Microsoft Update, and enables you to contact a support professional.
A legal agreement between Microsoft and the user, with regard to the terms of use and transfer of software.
A field data type that can contain up to 65,535 characters.
A communications device that converts between digital data from a computer or terminal and analog audio signals that can pass through a standard telephone line.
A feature of Microsoft Office that allows the user to view and edit image document formats like .TIF, which is the format most traditionally used in fax transmissions (IOs, T&Cs, etc.).
A Microsoft computer operating system with a command-line interface that is used to control many internal computer functions, such as running programs and organizing and maintaining files.
A program that shows details about a computer's hardware configuration, computer components, and software, including drivers.
An apartment that consists of one or more threads.
Pertaining to an operator that can hold an arbitrary number of arguments, or to a function that can hold an arbitrary number of operators.
In information processing, fixed or predetermined. For example, a static memory buffer remains invariant in size throughout program execution.
The direction a publication will be printed on a sheet of paper. You can choose either portrait, which is the taller-than-wide orientation, or landscape, which is the wider-than-tall orientation.
Pertaining to an operator that can hold an arbitrary number of arguments, or to a function that can hold an arbitrary number of operators.
Pertaining to the device, program, file, record or portion of the screen that is currently operational or subject to command operations.
Interactive or animated content used on the Internet. Active content includes ActiveX controls and web browser add-ons.
The document in which you are working.
The field on a form in which the cursor or pointer is positioned.
The window in which a user is currently working or directing input. An active window is typically at the top of the Z order and is distinguished by the color of its title bar.

A Windows feature that enables users to connect computers with a simple tap gesture or by browsing for computers within wireless range. If two computers come within 3-4 centimeters of each other, or are tapped together, the operating system of each computer becomes aware of the other computer. Also, a user can browse for computers within wireless range that are advertising for a peer connection. The user can then connect the two computers to share content such as photos or links, or create a multi-player experience. If the connection is made using a tap gesture, the user can also publish and subscribe to messages transmitted during the tap.
A network within an organization that uses Internet technologies and protocols, but is available only to certain people, such as employees of a company.

A server located on a network between client software, such as a Web browser, and another server. It intercepts all requests to the server to determine whether it can fulfill them itself. If not, it forwards the request to another server.
A type of sensor that can determine without physical contact if an object/person is nearby, or can be used to measure the distance between the object/person and the sensor device.
To enlarge a selected portion of a graphical image or document to fill a window or the screen.

A process that allows for the release of contents–for example emails and their attachments, address books, and contact lists–to the next of kin of a deceased or incapacitated email account holder and/or for the closure of an email account.
A feature that identifies people using computers near you so that you can use features and programs that use peer-to-peer technology.
A feature that allows the user to share items such as documents or folders with other devices on a layered communications network that operate on the same protocol level.
The data transfer capacity, or speed of transmission, of a digital communications system as measured in bits per second (bps).
To modify or assemble hardware or software to suit the needs or preferences of the user.
Erroneous or unrecognizable because of a flaw in reasoning or an error in input. Invalid results, for example, might occur if the logic in a program is faulty.
The inability to perform two or more activities because they have been scheduled to occur during the same time period and with the same resource or resources.
A feature that allows you to open any XAML file on your hard disk (or a hyperlink on a web page) and run it within a browser without compilation.
Of, pertaining to, or characteristic of communications that take place without the use of interconnecting wires or cables, such as by radio, microwave, or infrared.
A display that is connected through wireless communication.

A local area network (LAN) that sends and receives data via radio, infrared optical signaling, or some other technology that does not require a physical connection between individual nodes and the hub. Wireless LANs are often used in office or factory settings where a user must carry a portable computer from place to place.
A file that contains the data that a program will process and store in a destination file.
The original plan for a project, a work package, or an activity.

A statement indicating that the copyright holder reserves, or holds for their own use, all the rights provided by copyright law, such as distribution, performance, and creation of derivative works; that is, they have not waived any such right.

An area of storage on a hard disk. A volume is formatted by using a file system, such as NTFS, and has a drive letter assigned to it. A single hard disk can have multiple volumes. Some volumes can span multiple hard disks.

A Regional and Language Options setting that specifies the default code pages and associated bitmap font files for a specific computer that affects all of that computer's users. The default code pages and fonts enable a non-Unicode application written for one operating system language version to run correctly on another operating system language version.
In reference to a computing device or a program, unable to communicate with a primary database or controlling computer.
A network status that indicates a client is not connected to a server.
Help that is available to the user while not being connected to or part of a system or network.
To enlarge or reduce the display of an item, such as a drawing or a proportional character font, by adjusting its size proportionally.
To change the size of an element on the screen.
A listing of keywords and associated data that point to the location of more comprehensive information, such as files and records on a disk or record keys in a database.
To create an index.
To locate data based on an index.
In computer graphics, a display method in which a viewing window on the screen scans horizontally or vertically, like a camera, to bring offscreen extensions of the current image smoothly into view.

The feature of Bing that enables customers to block sites containing adult (sexually explicit) content. Customers can choose from three levels of filtering: strict (hides adult text and image results); moderate (hides adult images only); and off (doesn't hide any search results).
To move an application into the store. Involves the uploading and certification of an app's packages and listing data.
The set of records that results from running a query or applying a filter.
Pertaining to the state of an object when it is not the focus of a user's input.

In an environment capable of displaying multiple on-screen windows, any window other than the one currently being used for work. An inactive window can be partially or entirely hidden behind another window, and it remains inactive until the user selects it.
For local area networks (LANs), a device that is connected to the network and is capable of communicating with other network devices.
A location in a hierarchy (often a tree structure) that can have links to one or more nodes below it.
To publish or write entries for a blog.
A tab in Windows Media Player that enables the user to view the digital media item that is currently playing.
A software that was installed on the system without user's consent.
A variable-length data type that can hold a maximum of 2^30 - 1 (1,073,741,823) characters.

An advanced file system designed for use specifically with the Windows NT operating system. It supports long filenames, full security access control, file system recovery, extremely large storage media, and various features for the Windows NT POSIX subsystem. It also supports object-oriented applications by treating all files as objects with user-defined and system-defined attributes.
The value of a scorecard element in relation to the values of other elements of the same type.
A numeric or other designation appended to a product name to identify a particular release.
A feature that tracks and stores a record of a shared document when it is first created, and each time it is edited.

A slide view or page on which you define formatting for all slides or pages in your presentation. Each presentation has a master for each key component: slides, title slides, speaker's notes, and audience handouts.
A shape on a stencil that you use repeatedly to create drawings.
A storage pool that exposes physical disks that are currently available for inclusion in a storage pool on a particular storage subsystem.
A copy of a master, which you create by dragging the master from a stencil to a drawing.
The wider of the two apps that can appear on the screen simultaneously after a second app is snapped to the screen.
Any of the set of words that comprises a given programming language or set of operating-system routines.
A characteristic word, phrase, or code that is used to conduct sorting or searching operations.
A shape on a stencil that you use repeatedly to create drawings. When you drag a shape from a stencil onto the drawing page, the shape becomes an instance of that master.

A page that contains the design and layout elements that you want to repeat on multiple pages in a publication. Using master pages for these common elements gives your publication a more consistent appearance.
A personal record of experiences, occurrances or proceedings, like a Baby Book or Life Book.
A record of transactions or events that take place within an IT managed environment.

A file that stores messages generated by an application, service, or operating system. These messages are used to track the operations performed. Log files are usually plain text (ASCII) files and often have a .log extension.
A set of scenarios that lead to a desired outcome by a customer segment.
Pertaining to a value that indicates missing or unknown data.

The condition at a particular time of any of numerous elements of computing--a device, a communications channel, a network station, a program, a bit, or other element--used to report on or to control computer operations.
A set of items or devices working together to accomplish a task.
Any application or process that you want to model. Examples of systems include a computer game, a process for assembling microchips, and a library information system.
A Windows technology that protects OS resources (files, folders registry keys) and prevents applications and administrators from making changes to a computer's resources in an arbitrary way.
A Windows feature that enables users to easily roll back their PC to an earlier good state while keeping their data safe.
A program that shows details about a computer's hardware configuration, computer components, and software, including drivers.
The information about a computer that's derived from the System Information program.
